perturbation.original_text: 14.3 Choice of Law This Agreement will be governed by the substantive laws of the State of New York, without regard for its conflict of laws provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: 14.3 Choice of Law This Agreement will be governed by the laws of a jurisdiction selected by IBM.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the explicit reference to New York law and replaces it with a clause that allows IBM to select the governing jurisdiction, which could potentially be a jurisdiction with less consumer protection. This could allow IBM to avoid consumer protection laws in New York, where Bluefly, Inc. is located.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GBL § 349 prohibits deceptive acts and practices in business. By allowing IBM to select a jurisdiction with weaker consumer protection laws, this clause could enable deceptive practices that would be illegal under New York law, thus violating NY GBL § 349.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies that New York law governs the agreement. The changed text allows IBM to select any jurisdiction, potentially one with weaker consumer protections, contradicting the initial agreement to abide by New York law. The Law Explanation notes that this could enable deceptive practices illegal under NY GBL § 349. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Article 22-A regarding consumer protection from deceptive acts and practices under New York law. By removing the New York law stipulation, the contract potentially undermines these consumer protections, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 14.4 Waiver of Jury Trial The parties waive any right to a jury trial in any proceeding arising out of or related to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 14.4 Waiver of Jury Trial Customer waives any right to a jury trial in any proceeding arising out of or related to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause that IBM *also* waives the right to a jury trial, this creates an imbalance and potentially violates principles of unconscionability, particularly if the customer is not sophisticated. It removes a right from the consumer but retains it for the corporation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 4102
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 4102 outlines the procedure for waiving a jury trial. While not explicitly prohibited, a one-sided waiver raises concerns about fairness and whether the waiver was knowing and voluntary, especially in contracts of adhesion. This modification introduces an imbalance that increases the risk of the waiver being deemed unenforceable under New York law.

Demand and waiver of trial by jury; specification of issues. (a) Demand. Any party may demand a trial by jury of any issue of fact triable of right by a jury, by serving upon all other parties and filing a note of issue containing a demand for trial by jury. Any party served with a note of issue not containing such a demand may demand a trial by jury by serving upon each party a demand for a trial by jury and filing such demand in the office where the note of issue was filed within fifteen days after service of the note of issue. A demand shall not be accepted for filing unless a note of issue is filed in the action. If no party shall demand a trial by jury as provided herein, the right to trial by jury shall be deemed waived by all parties. A party may not withdraw a demand for trial by jury without the consent of the other parties, regardless of whether another party previously filed a note of issue without a demand for trial by jury. (b) Specification of issues. In his demand a party may specify the issues which he wishes tried by jury; otherwise he shall be deemed to have demanded trial by jury of all issues so triable. If he has demanded trial by jury of only some of the issues, any other party within ten days after service of the demand may serve and file a demand for trial by jury of any other issues in the action so triable. (c) Waiver. A party who has demanded the trial of an issue of fact by a jury under this section waives his right by failing to appear at the trial, by filing a written waiver with the clerk or by oral waiver in open court. A waiver does not withdraw a demand for trial by jury without the consent of the other parties. A party shall not be deemed to have waived the right to trial by jury of the issues of fact arising upon a claim, by joining it with another claim with respect to which there is no right to trial by jur
perturbation.justification: The modification omits IBM's waiver of jury trial, creating an imbalance. The original text ensures both parties waive this right, while the changed text only binds the customer, potentially violating principles of unconscionability, especially if the customer is unsophisticated. The provided law snippet, NY CPLR § 4102, addresses 'Demand and waiver of trial by jury.' While it doesn't explicitly prohibit a one-sided waiver, the Law Explanation highlights concerns about fairness and whether the waiver was knowing and voluntary, especially in contracts of adhesion. This imbalance increases the risk of the waiver being deemed unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 14.18 Data Protection You agree to allow International Business Machines Corporation and entities within its Enterprise to store and use your contact information, including names, phone numbers, and e-mail addresses, anywhere they do business. Such information will be processed and used in connection with our business relationship, and may be provided to contractors, Business Partners, and assignees of IBM Corp. and entities within its Enterprise for uses consistent with their collective business activities, including communicating with you (for example, for processing orders, for promotions, and for marketing research). For personal information processed by IBM on your behalf as part of the Services, IBM will act in accordance with your instructions by following such processing and security obligations as are contained in this Agreement. You also confirm that you are solely responsible for ensuring that any processing and security obligations comply with applicable data protection laws. Your contact information shall not be considered personal information processed on your behalf.
perturbation.changed_text: 14.18 Data Protection You agree to allow International Business Machines Corporation and entities within its Enterprise to store and use your contact information, including names, phone numbers, and e-mail addresses, anywhere they do business. Such information will be processed and used in connection with our business relationship, and may be provided to contractors, Business Partners, and assignees of IBM Corp. and entities within its Enterprise for uses consistent with their collective business activities, including communicating with you (for example, for processing orders, for promotions, and for marketing research). Your contact information shall not be considered personal information processed on your behalf.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the sentences 'For personal information processed by IBM on your behalf as part of the Services, IBM will act in accordance with your instructions by following such processing and security obligations as are contained in this Agreement. You also confirm that you are solely responsible for ensuring that any processing and security obligations comply with applicable data protection laws.', the contract absolves IBM of any data security responsibility with the customer, and the customer is solely responsible for the obligations. This introduces legal risk in the form of potential violations of data protection laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Information Security Breach and Notification Act - General Business Law § 899-AA
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GBL § 899-AA requires businesses to implement reasonable security measures to protect private information and to notify affected individuals in the event of a data breach. By removing IBM's explicit obligation to follow processing and security obligations, the contract could be interpreted as failing to meet the 'reasonable security measures' requirement, potentially violating NY GBL § 899-AA, especially if a breach occurs due to IBM's negligence. Though this is also a data transfer agreement, the lack of clarity as to the party responsible for information security measures creates a contradiction.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies that IBM will follow processing and security obligations contained within the agreement for personal information processed on the customer's behalf. It also confirms the customer is responsible for ensuring these obligations comply with data protection laws. The changed contract omits these sentences, shifting the burden of data protection and security obligations solely onto the customer. The Law Explanation states that NY GBL § 899-AA requires businesses to implement reasonable security measures to protect private information. By removing IBM's obligation to follow processing and security obligations, the contract could be interpreted as failing to meet these 'reasonable security measures' requirements, especially if a breach occurs due to IBM's negligence, thus contradicting the law.
